mechanism_description: The City will enable net-zero-carbon construction, reducing emissions from building construction and associated materials.
primary_causal_pathway: Construction practices/material choices → ↓ embodied + construction-phase GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Building project set and market practices shift toward net-zero-carbon construction (e.g., low-carbon concrete/steel, recycled/biobased materials where appropriate, design optimization to use less material, electrified construction equipment, cleaner construction energy, and carbon accounting/verification).  
(2) Process: Reduced upstream industrial emissions (cement clinker production, steelmaking, material transport) and reduced on-site combustion from construction equipment; potentially increased use of lower-carbon supply chains.  
(3) Outcome: Lower embodied and construction-phase CO₂e per building (and across the building stock as new builds/major projects proceed), reducing total community-wide GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering life-cycle GHG emissions by reducing embodied/carbon-intensive construction materials and construction-phase energy/fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The policy mechanism primarily reduces GHG emissions by lowering embodied and construction-stage emissions associated with materials and construction activities.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some implementations may also improve operational efficiency or include carbon storage in biogenic materials, the mechanism as stated (“net-zero-carbon construction… reducing emissions from building construction and associated materials”) is fundamentally an emissions-reduction (mitigation) pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “net-zero” is achieved largely via offsets rather than real embodied-emissions cuts, the direct physical mitigation impact is weaker; however, the mechanism’s intent and typical implementation still targets emissions reductions in the construction/materials system. If the policy emphasizes wood/carbon-storing materials, there can be a nature-based co-benefit, but the dominant effect remains mitigation via reduced CO₂e.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism necessarily aims to reduce construction/material-related GHG emissions (embodied + construction-phase), which is mitigation regardless of jurisdiction.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the primary lever is low-carbon concrete, buy-clean procurement, electrified equipment, design for material efficiency, or offsets/verification is policy-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy-evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity if “net-zero-carbon construction” is met primarily through offsets/RECs rather than measured embodied-carbon reductions; some versions may blend operational net-zero with embodied-carbon goals, but the stated mechanism centers on construction/material emissions.
key_indicators: "Enable net-zero-carbon construction"; "by 2040"; "in alignment with ... Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change"
instance_edge_case_notes: “Enable” is ambiguous (could imply future regulatory changes, incentives, or technical assistance), but no specific instrument is described in the text; classification therefore defaults to target-setting rather than a mandate/performance standard.
